_meta.authoringNotes: Citations cross-verified against WHO 2023 shigellosis position paper, IDSA 2017 infectious diarrhea guideline (Shane, Mody, Crump et al., CID 65:e45-e80, PMID 29053792), CDC Pink Book chapter on Shigella, GBD 2019 diarrheal disease analysis (Khalil 2021, Lancet Infectious Diseases). Antibiotic resistance data drawn from WHO GLASS 2022 and CDC NARMS surveillance.

h1Title: Shigellosis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Shigellosis is an acute bacterial intestinal infection caused by Shigella species, transmitted through the fecal-oral route and notable for one of the lowest infectious doses of any enteric pathogen — as few as 10 to 100 organisms can cause illness. The hallmark presentation is bloody, mucoid diarrhea with fever, severe abdominal cramps, and the urgent painful straining known as tenesmus. The World Health Organization estimates 80 million symptomatic cases and roughly 600,000 deaths globally each year, with the heaviest burden falling on children under five in low- and middle-income countries. In the United States, the CDC tracks about 450,000 infections annually, mostly caused by Shigella sonnei. Most healthy adults recover within five to seven days with oral rehydration alone, but rising multidrug resistance has reshaped treatment guidance worldwide.

keyStats.prevalence: ~80 million symptomatic cases worldwide each year (Khalil 2021, GBD); ~450,000 US infections annually per CDC FoodNet estimates
keyStats.demographics: Heaviest disease burden in children under 5 in low- and middle-income countries; outbreaks also concentrate in men who have sex with men, daycare attendees, and travelers
keyStats.avgAge: Bimodal — peaks in children 1-4 years globally; second peak in adults 20-49 years in high-income outbreak settings
keyStats.globalCases: ~600,000 annual deaths globally, with 60-70% in children under 5 (Khalil 2021)

definition: Shigellosis (ICD-10: A03.9) is an acute invasive bacterial enterocolitis caused by four species of the genus Shigella — S. sonnei, S. flexneri, S. boydii, and S. dysenteriae — facultative anaerobic Gram-negative rods within the family Enterobacteriaceae. The organisms invade and replicate within the colonic epithelium, triggering intense inflammation, mucosal ulceration, and the dysenteric syndrome of bloody mucoid stools, fever, and tenesmus. S. sonnei accounts for the majority of cases in high-income countries; S. flexneri dominates in low- and middle-income settings; S. dysenteriae type 1 produces Shiga toxin and historically caused devastating epidemics in displaced populations. Shigella has one of the lowest infectious doses of any enteric pathogen — 10 to 100 organisms suffice — which explains rapid person-to-person spread in households, daycare centers, residential institutions, and among men who have sex with men. The bacterium is restricted to humans and higher primates; there is no animal reservoir. Recognized as a priority antimicrobial-resistance pathogen by both the WHO and CDC, shigellosis is reportable to public health authorities in most countries. Management is led by primary care or pediatrics for uncomplicated cases, with infectious-disease specialists involved for severe, prolonged, or resistant infections.

typesClassification.description: Group D species responsible for over 75% of US and most European cases. Tends to produce milder watery-to-bloody diarrhea, often without overt dysentery. Increasingly multidrug-resistant in Western outbreaks.

Group B species dominant in South Asia, sub-Saharan Africa, and Latin America. Causes classic dysentery with frequent bloody mucoid stools, fever, and high rates of complications in malnourished children.
Group C species, geographically restricted mainly to the Indian subcontinent. Rare globally; clinical course resembles S. flexneri but is generally milder.

Group A species. Type 1 produces Shiga toxin, the most severe form of shigellosis. Associated with large epidemics in refugee and conflict settings and with hemolytic uremic syndrome (HUS). Now rare in high-income countries.

primarySymptoms: Frequent diarrhea that begins watery and progresses over 24-48 hours to small-volume stools containing visible blood and mucus, occurring 10-30 times per day in severe cases.
Sudden cramping lower abdominal pain that intensifies before each bowel movement and partially eases afterward.
Tenesmus — a painful, urgent, persistent feeling of needing to pass stool even when the rectum is empty, often producing only a small amount of bloody mucus.
Fever, typically 38.5-40°C (101-104°F), appearing within the first 24-48 hours and lasting 2-3 days in most patients.
Profound fatigue, malaise, and reduced appetite that can persist for a week beyond resolution of diarrhea.
Nausea and occasional vomiting, more prominent in children, although vomiting is less central than in viral gastroenteritis.
Signs of dehydration in severe cases — dry mouth, dizziness on standing, reduced urine output, and in children sunken eyes or fontanelle.
Rectal prolapse in young children with high stool frequency and intense straining, especially in undernourished settings.
Headache, confusion, or febrile seizures in children — Shigella is one of the more common bacterial causes of febrile seizure in toddlers.
Resolution of acute symptoms over 5-7 days in untreated immunocompetent adults; longer in young children, the elderly, and immunocompromised patients.
earlyWarningSigns: Sudden onset of watery diarrhea within 1-4 days of a known exposure (childcare outbreak, travel to an endemic region, sexual contact with an infected partner)
Lower abdominal cramps that worsen with each bowel movement
Blood streaking or visible mucus in stool, even before frank bloody diarrhea develops
emergencySigns: Signs of severe dehydration — lethargy, sunken eyes, minimal urine output, rapid weak pulse, or capillary refill over 3 seconds
High fever with confusion, seizure, or stiff neck (suspect bacteremia or Shigella encephalopathy)
Decreasing urine output with pallor, easy bruising, or dark cola-colored urine appearing 5-10 days into illness (possible hemolytic uremic syndrome from S. dysenteriae 1)
Severe abdominal distension, absent bowel sounds, or peritoneal signs suggesting toxic megacolon or perforation
Bloody diarrhea in an infant under 6 months, in pregnancy, or in anyone immunocompromised

causes.cause: Fecal-oral transmission from a contaminated person
causes.description: Direct person-to-person spread is the dominant route, especially in households, daycare, residential institutions, and during sex involving oral-anal contact. The very low infectious dose (10-100 organisms) makes hand hygiene the single most important interruption point.

Outbreaks are repeatedly traced to food handled by an infected person with poor hand hygiene — salads, sandwiches, and ready-to-eat items predominate. Less commonly, raw produce irrigated with contaminated water is implicated.
Drinking water, recreational water (swimming pools, splash pads, lakes), and water used to wash produce can transmit Shigella when sewage contamination occurs and chlorination is inadequate.

Sexually transmitted shigellosis — particularly among men who have sex with men — has become a significant route in high-income countries, often involving multidrug-resistant or extensively drug-resistant S. sonnei and S. flexneri strains.

Travellers' diarrhea in South Asia, sub-Saharan Africa, and parts of Latin America frequently involves Shigella. Returning travellers can seed local outbreaks if hygiene measures are not observed during the symptomatic period.
Open defecation, lack of safe water, and overcrowding sustain high background transmission in low- and middle-income countries and in refugee or displacement settings.

riskFactors.description: Children under 5 carry the majority of global shigellosis morbidity and mortality. Immature immunity, hand-to-mouth behavior, and crowded childcare settings drive both incidence and severity.

riskFactors.factor: Living in or travelling to low- or middle-income countries
riskFactors.description: Inadequate sanitation, unsafe drinking water, and crowded living conditions sustain background transmission. South Asia and sub-Saharan Africa carry the highest burden.

riskFactors.factor: Attendance at or work in childcare centers
riskFactors.description: Diapering, shared toys, and hand-to-mouth behavior in toddlers produce a transmission rate per exposure higher than for almost any other enteric pathogen.

riskFactors.factor: Men who have sex with men (MSM)
riskFactors.description: Sexual practices involving oral-anal contact have driven sustained transmission of multidrug-resistant Shigella in many high-income cities since 2015 (CDC HAN 444, 2022).
Prisons, shelters, and long-term care facilities experience explosive outbreaks once a case is introduced, owing to shared bathrooms and the low infectious dose.
Advanced HIV (CD4 <200) raises risk of bacteremic, prolonged, and relapsing Shigella infection. Stem-cell and solid-organ transplant recipients are similarly susceptible.
Stunted and wasted children are both more likely to acquire Shigella and more likely to develop persistent diarrhea, severe dehydration, and post-infectious enteropathy.
Proton-pump inhibitors and H2 blockers raise gastric pH and modestly increase susceptibility to all infectious diarrheas, including shigellosis.
Antibiotic-induced gut microbiome disruption can lower the infectious dose required to produce illness on subsequent exposure.

affectedDemographics: Globally, 60-70% of shigellosis deaths occur in children under 5 (Khalil 2021, GBD 2019)
In the US, S. sonnei accounts for over 75% of isolates; rates are highest among children 1-4 years and adults 20-39 years
Multidrug-resistant Shigella (resistant to azithromycin, ciprofloxacin, and ceftriaxone) accounted for 5% of US isolates in 2022, up from <1% in 2015 (CDC NARMS)
MSM-associated outbreaks have driven extensively drug-resistant S. sonnei clusters in the UK, Europe, North America, and Australia
Pediatric rates in sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia routinely exceed 100 cases per 1,000 child-years in surveillance studies (GEMS, MAL-ED)
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis combines clinical suspicion with laboratory confirmation. Any patient with acute bloody or mucoid diarrhea, fever, and tenesmus should be evaluated for Shigella alongside other invasive enteric pathogens (Salmonella, Campylobacter, Shiga toxin-producing E. coli, Entamoeba histolytica). The historical gold standard is stool culture on selective media (MacConkey, Hektoen enteric, XLD agar), which both confirms the species and supports antimicrobial susceptibility testing — essential in an era of rapidly rising resistance. IDSA 2017 guidance recommends culture, susceptibility testing, or both for any patient with bloody diarrhea, severe disease, immunocompromise, suspected outbreak involvement, or contact with high-risk settings. Multiplex PCR gastrointestinal panels (BioFire FilmArray, Verigene, Luminex xTAG) detect Shigella DNA within hours and have largely replaced microscopy in high-income laboratories, with sensitivities above 95%. However, PCR cannot distinguish Shigella from enteroinvasive E. coli (which share the ipaH gene) and yields no isolate for susceptibility testing — culture should be requested in parallel. Stool microscopy commonly shows abundant fecal leukocytes and red blood cells, supportive but non-specific. Blood cultures are indicated in severely ill, immunocompromised, or septic-appearing patients, especially in regions where S. dysenteriae 1 circulates. In suspected outbreaks, whole-genome sequencing performed by public health laboratories links cases, identifies resistance genes, and informs empiric therapy choices. Stool ova-and-parasite examination should also be considered in returning travelers to exclude Entamoeba histolytica, the principal alternative cause of bloody diarrhea in tropical settings.

diagnosticTests.test: Stool culture with antimicrobial susceptibility testing
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms Shigella species and provides the antibiotic susceptibility pattern required to choose effective therapy in an era of rapidly rising multidrug resistance. Remains the reference standard recommended by IDSA and WHO.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A fresh stool sample is collected at home or in clinic and transported to the lab in a Cary-Blair or similar transport medium. Results typically return in 48-72 hours; susceptibility data in another 24 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Rapid simultaneous detection of Shigella and other diarrheal pathogens (Salmonella, Campylobacter, Shiga-toxin E. coli, norovirus, Giardia, Cryptosporidium, Entamoeba histolytica). Sensitivity over 95% for Shigella DNA.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Stool sample processed by the laboratory; results often available within 2-6 hours of receipt. Note that PCR alone does not distinguish Shigella from enteroinvasive E. coli.

diagnosticTests.test: Stool microscopy for leukocytes and red blood cells
diagnosticTests.purpose: Supports a diagnosis of invasive bacterial colitis when many polymorphonuclear leukocytes and erythrocytes are seen, helping distinguish it from non-invasive viral or toxigenic diarrhea.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small portion of stool is examined under a microscope after methylene-blue or Wright staining. Results return same day. Non-specific — confirms inflammation but not the organism.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies Entamoeba histolytica, the chief differential for bloody diarrhea in returning travelers and immigrants from endemic regions. Three sequential samples improve sensitivity.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Three stool samples collected over consecutive days. Microscopic examination by trained parasitologists; results within 1-3 days. Antigen testing for E. histolytica improves accuracy where available.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Indicated in severely ill, septic-appearing, immunocompromised, or malnourished pediatric patients to detect Shigella bacteremia, which carries higher mortality and requires intravenous antibiotics.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Two sets of blood drawn from separate venipuncture sites and incubated in the lab. Preliminary results at 24-48 hours; full identification and susceptibility by 72 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Serum chemistry and complete blood count
diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses dehydration, electrolyte disturbance (hyponatremia, hypokalemia, acidosis), kidney function, and signs of hemolytic uremic syndrome (falling hemoglobin, falling platelets, rising creatinine) in S. dysenteriae 1 infection.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard venous blood draw. Results within hours. Repeat testing is often needed in severe or pediatric cases.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Reserved for prolonged or atypical cases where the differential includes inflammatory bowel disease, ischemic colitis, or amebic colitis. Reveals diffuse erythematous mucosa with shallow ulcerations in shigellosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Outpatient procedure with bowel preparation and sedation; targeted biopsies are sent for histology and culture. Not used for routine acute shigellosis.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of shigellosis rests on three pillars: aggressive rehydration, judicious use of antibiotics, and infection control. Oral rehydration with WHO-formula oral rehydration solution (low-osmolarity ORS) is the cornerstone for every patient who can drink, replacing fluid and electrolyte losses across the spectrum of severity. Intravenous fluids — typically Ringer's lactate — are reserved for patients with severe dehydration, persistent vomiting, or shock. Antimicrobial therapy shortens illness by 1-3 days, reduces stool shedding (and thereby onward transmission), and lowers complication rates; both IDSA 2017 and WHO 2017 recommend antibiotic treatment for confirmed Shigella infection in most patients, with the strongest indication in severe disease, dysentery, hospitalization, immunocompromise, age extremes, food handlers, healthcare workers, and childcare attendees or staff. First-line therapy globally is azithromycin (500 mg orally daily for 3 days in adults; 10 mg/kg daily for 3 days in children), reflecting widespread fluoroquinolone resistance especially in returning travelers, MSM-associated outbreaks, and South Asia. Ciprofloxacin (500 mg twice daily for 3 days) remains an alternative where local susceptibility supports it. Severe or invasive disease, neonates, and patients with bacteremia receive intravenous ceftriaxone (50-75 mg/kg/day in children; 1-2 g daily in adults) for 5 days. Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole and ampicillin are no longer reliable empirically because of resistance rates above 50% in most surveillance programs. Loperamide and other antimotility agents should be avoided in dysentery — they may prolong illness and have been associated with toxic megacolon and increased risk of hemolytic uremic syndrome in S. dysenteriae 1. Probiotics have not demonstrated reliable benefit in shigellosis specifically and are not part of standard guidelines. Zinc supplementation (20 mg daily for 10-14 days in children over 6 months, 10 mg in infants under 6 months) is part of WHO-recommended pediatric diarrhea management and reduces duration and recurrence. Public-health measures — exclusion from work or school until 48 hours after symptom resolution for high-risk occupations, food-handler clearance with negative stool cultures, contact tracing, and notification of public health authorities — are integral to treatment in confirmed cases.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral rehydration solution (low-osmolarity WHO formula)
medicalTreatments.description: Replaces water and electrolytes lost through diarrhea. Used in every patient who can drink. Continued as long as diarrhea persists. Volumes are guided by ongoing losses — roughly 50-100 mL after each loose stool in adults, 10 mL/kg in children.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces shigellosis mortality from severe dehydration by more than 90% when used early; backbone of WHO and IDSA management.

medicalTreatments.name: Azithromycin (500 mg orally daily for 3 days in adults; 10 mg/kg daily for 3 days in children)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line antimicrobial in most high-income and many endemic settings due to rising ciprofloxacin resistance. Effective against the great majority of S. sonnei and S. flexneri isolates. WHO AWaRe Watch group antibiotic.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Shortens symptomatic illness by approximately 2 days and reduces stool shedding (Christopher 2010 Cochrane review of antibiotics for shigellosis).

medicalTreatments.name: Ciprofloxacin (500 mg orally twice daily for 3 days; 15 mg/kg twice daily in children where indicated)
medicalTreatments.description: Alternative first-line therapy where local susceptibility data confirm activity. Use is limited by rising resistance, particularly in South Asia, the Eastern Mediterranean, and MSM-associated outbreaks. Avoid in patients with known fluoroquinolone allergy or QT prolongation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Resolution of dysentery within 48-72 hours in susceptible isolates; failure rates rise sharply with reduced susceptibility (MIC 0.12-0.5 µg/mL).

medicalTreatments.name: Ceftriaxone (50-75 mg/kg/day IV for children, 1-2 g/day IV for adults, for 5 days)
medicalTreatments.description: Drug of choice for severe shigellosis requiring hospitalization, bacteremia, immunocompromised patients, neonates, and patients unable to tolerate oral therapy. Effective against most strains, including many multidrug-resistant isolates.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Resolution of bacteremia and severe dysentery in over 90% of susceptible cases. Reserve use to preserve activity against extensively drug-resistant strains.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous Ringer's lactate or normal saline
medicalTreatments.description: Used for severe dehydration, hypovolemic shock, or persistent vomiting. WHO recommends Ringer's lactate at 30 mL/kg over the first 30 minutes (1 hour in infants), then 70 mL/kg over the next 2.5 hours (5 hours in infants), with reassessment.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Life-saving in severe dehydration; rapidly restores perfusion and reduces mortality to under 1% in well-equipped settings.

medicalTreatments.name: Zinc supplementation (20 mg/day in children over 6 months; 10 mg/day in infants under 6 months, for 10-14 days)
medicalTreatments.description: Recommended by WHO and UNICEF for all childhood diarrhea in low- and middle-income countries. Shortens diarrhea duration and reduces relapse within the following 2-3 months.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces duration of diarrhea by approximately 25% and recurrence by approximately 20% (Lancet 2010 Cochrane review of zinc for childhood diarrhea).

medicalTreatments.name: Pivmecillinam (400 mg orally four times daily for 5 days, adults)
medicalTreatments.description: Oral aminopenicillin used in regions where it is available (mainly Europe and parts of Asia). Useful alternative in azithromycin- or ciprofloxacin-resistant isolates with confirmed susceptibility.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical cure in 80-90% of susceptible cases (WHO 2005 guidelines for shigellosis).

surgicalOptions.name: Surgical management of toxic megacolon
surgicalOptions.description: Reserved for the rare patient who develops toxic megacolon or colonic perforation despite optimal medical therapy. Subtotal colectomy with end ileostomy is the typical operation. Largely confined to severe S. dysenteriae 1 infection and immunocompromised hosts.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Survival approaches 70-80% with early surgical intervention; mortality rises steeply with delay.

surgicalOptions.name: Rectal prolapse reduction in children
surgicalOptions.description: Persistent rectal prolapse from severe straining may need manual reduction under sedation and, rarely, surgical fixation if it recurs after the acute illness has resolved.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Manual reduction succeeds in most cases; surgical fixation is needed in fewer than 5% of pediatric shigellosis.

alternativeTreatments.name: Probiotic supplementation (Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG, Saccharomyces boulardii)
alternativeTreatments.description: Studied in pediatric acute diarrhea broadly; evidence specifically in shigellosis is limited and not part of current IDSA or WHO guidelines. May modestly shorten duration of mixed acute diarrhea in some trials but should not replace rehydration or appropriate antibiotics.

alternativeTreatments.name: Bismuth subsalicylate
alternativeTreatments.description: Reduces stool frequency in non-bloody travelers' diarrhea but is not recommended in dysenteric or bloody shigellosis, where antimotility-style effects could prolong invasion or precipitate complications. Avoid in children due to salicylate toxicity risk.

whySeeSpecialist: Most uncomplicated shigellosis is managed in primary care or pediatric clinics with oral rehydration and short-course antibiotics where indicated. Infectious-disease consultation is appropriate for severe or hospitalized patients, bacteremia, immunocompromised hosts, infection in pregnancy, multidrug-resistant or extensively drug-resistant isolates, failure of first-line therapy after 48-72 hours, suspected hemolytic uremic syndrome, and outbreak investigation. Public health departments should be notified for every laboratory-confirmed case and assist with contact tracing, food-handler clearance, and outbreak response.
doctorSelectionGuide: For severe or refractory disease, look for an infectious-disease physician with access to the local antibiogram, experience with multidrug-resistant enteric infections, and a working relationship with the public health laboratory. In children, a pediatric infectious-disease specialist is preferred. For travelers and MSM-associated infection, clinics with experience in travel medicine or sexual-health medicine often have the most current resistance data and can coordinate partner notification.
preventionStrategies: Wash hands with soap and water for at least 20 seconds after using the toilet, after changing diapers, before preparing food, and before eating — this is the single most effective intervention

Exclude symptomatic individuals from childcare, food handling, healthcare work, and care of vulnerable patients until 48 hours after diarrhea resolves and, for food handlers, until cleared by negative stool cultures per local public health rules
Use only safely treated water for drinking, brushing teeth, and washing produce when traveling in regions of high transmission
Avoid raw and undercooked food prepared in settings with uncertain hygiene; choose items served hot, fruit you peel yourself, and bottled or boiled water
Practice safer sex measures (barriers for oral-anal contact, hand washing after sexual activity) to reduce sexually transmitted shigellosis

Vaccinate against rotavirus in childhood and stay up to date on routine immunizations — while there is no licensed Shigella vaccine yet, candidate vaccines are in advanced clinical trials and may be available in the next several years
lifestyleModifications: Maintain a quiet diet during recovery — bananas, rice, applesauce, toast, lean protein, and broth-based soups while the gut heals
Avoid alcohol, large fatty meals, and caffeine while diarrhea persists, as these can worsen symptoms
Reintroduce dairy cautiously — transient lactose intolerance is common for 1-2 weeks after invasive enteric infections
Continue breastfeeding infants throughout the illness; breast milk provides both nutrition and immune protection
Wash all bedding, towels, and clothing soiled with stool in hot water with detergent, and clean bathroom surfaces with a chlorine-based disinfectant
Stay home from work, school, or childcare for at least 48 hours after the last episode of diarrhea, even after antibiotics have started, to prevent ongoing transmission

recommended: Oral rehydration solution sipped frequently — small volumes every 5-10 minutes are better tolerated than large drinks
Bananas, rice, applesauce, toast (the BRAT diet) for the first 24-48 hours of refeeding
Lean protein (chicken, fish, eggs, lentils) as appetite returns
Cooked vegetables, well-peeled fruit, and oatmeal
Plain yogurt with live cultures once tolerated — may modestly help microbiome recovery
Plenty of clean water throughout convalescence
avoid: Spicy and heavily fried foods that can irritate inflamed colon
Sugar-sweetened beverages and fruit juices, which can worsen osmotic diarrhea
Alcohol and caffeinated drinks during acute illness and the first week of recovery
Dairy in patients who notice bloating or recurrence of diarrhea with milk — transient lactose intolerance lasts 1-2 weeks
Raw shellfish, unpasteurized dairy, and street food during recovery to reduce risk of repeat enteric infection
Anti-diarrheal medications (loperamide, diphenoxylate) unless specifically directed by a clinician — they may prolong invasive colitis

exerciseGuidelines: Rest is appropriate during fever and active diarrhea; most patients are exhausted and dehydrated. Resume light activity once fever resolves and oral intake is steady, typically 3-5 days into recovery. Return to vigorous exercise gradually over the following 1-2 weeks, paying attention to hydration. Athletes should avoid intense training while still passing loose stools to prevent dehydration-related complications.
dailyManagement: Drink small amounts of oral rehydration solution every 5-10 minutes throughout the day during active diarrhea
Monitor temperature twice daily and seek care if fever exceeds 39.5°C or persists beyond 72 hours after antibiotics begin
Watch for warning signs — declining urine output, persistent vomiting, confusion, blood in urine, or bruising — and seek urgent care if they appear
Complete the prescribed antibiotic course in full, even if symptoms resolve early, to reduce shedding and resistance
Wash hands meticulously after every bathroom visit and before any food preparation; assume you remain contagious until at least 48 hours after symptoms stop
Inform close contacts, employers, and schools of the diagnosis so they can monitor for symptoms and apply local exclusion rules
prognosis: Prognosis for shigellosis in healthy adults with access to rehydration and appropriate antibiotics is excellent — over 95% recover fully within 5-7 days, and case-fatality rates in high-income settings are under 0.1%. Prognosis is poorer at the extremes of age, in malnutrition, in immunocompromise, and in regions with limited access to medical care, where children under 5 still account for the majority of the 600,000 annual global deaths. S. dysenteriae type 1 carries the highest historical mortality — up to 10% during epidemics — driven by hemolytic uremic syndrome and toxic megacolon. Post-infectious sequelae are a meaningful long tail: reactive arthritis develops in 1-3% of cases, post-infectious irritable bowel syndrome in 5-15%, and a chronic carrier state in fewer than 3% of patients. Antimicrobial resistance is the single greatest emerging threat — extensively drug-resistant S. sonnei strains have been associated with longer illness, more invasive disease, and increased transmission, and the WHO has placed Shigella on its priority pathogen list for new antibiotic development.
recoveryTimeline: Diarrhea typically peaks within 48 hours of symptom onset and improves over 3-5 days. With appropriate antibiotics, fever resolves within 24-48 hours and stool frequency normalizes by day 4-5. Fatigue and reduced appetite can persist for 1-2 weeks. Stool shedding of Shigella usually ceases within 1-2 weeks but can occasionally continue for 4 weeks; food handlers and healthcare workers should remain cleared until documented stool clearance per local rules. Most patients return to full activity within 7-14 days.
complications: Severe dehydration with hypovolemic shock — the most common cause of death globally, especially in young children
Hemolytic uremic syndrome (microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, acute kidney injury) in S. dysenteriae type 1 infection, developing 5-10 days into illness
Bacteremia and sepsis, more common in malnourished children, immunocompromised hosts, and neonates
Toxic megacolon and colonic perforation, rare but life-threatening, more frequent with antimotility agents
Reactive arthritis (post-infectious arthropathy with conjunctivitis and urethritis in some patients), developing in 1-3% of cases weeks after the acute episode
Post-infectious irritable bowel syndrome and chronic diarrhea, reported in 5-15% of patients on long-term follow-up
Febrile seizures in young children, occasionally with Shigella encephalopathy ('ekiri' syndrome) producing severe neurologic illness
Rectal prolapse in children with intense tenesmus, particularly in malnutrition
Persistent carriage in a small subset (under 3%) with ongoing transmission risk if hygiene is poor

supportResources.name: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention — Shigella
supportResources.description: US public health authority page with patient information, outbreak alerts, and clinician guidance.

supportResources.name: World Health Organization — Diarrhoeal disease and shigellosis
supportResources.description: Global epidemiology, treatment principles, and oral rehydration guidance from WHO.

supportResources.name: NHS — Shigella infection
supportResources.description: Patient-facing UK overview with symptoms, treatment, and prevention advice.

confusedWithConditions.name: E. coli Infection (including Shiga toxin-producing E. coli)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Shiga toxin-producing E. coli (STEC, including O157:H7) also causes bloody diarrhea and HUS but typically without high fever, and most STEC strains do not invade the mucosa as Shigella does. Stool culture or PCR distinguishes the two; antibiotics are generally avoided in STEC because they may increase HUS risk.

confusedWithConditions.name: Salmonella Infection
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Non-typhoidal Salmonella produces watery diarrhea with fever and crampy abdominal pain, but bloody stools and tenesmus are less prominent than in shigellosis, and the infectious dose is much higher (around 1 million organisms). Bacteremia is more common in Salmonella than in Shigella in adults.

confusedWithConditions.name: Amebiasis (Entamoeba histolytica)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Amebic dysentery causes bloody mucoid diarrhea but usually with a more gradual onset over 1-3 weeks, lower-grade fever, and risk of liver abscess. Stool ova-and-parasite examination, antigen testing, or PCR identifies the protozoan; treatment uses metronidazole, not azithromycin.

confusedWithConditions.name: Ulcerative Colitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Ulcerative colitis also produces bloody mucoid diarrhea and tenesmus but develops gradually over weeks to months, lacks the typical acute fever spike, and shows characteristic chronic inflammation on colonoscopy with biopsy. Stool cultures and PCR for Shigella are negative.

confusedWithConditions.name: Crohn's Disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Crohn's can cause bloody diarrhea, weight loss, and abdominal pain over months, often with perianal disease or extraintestinal manifestations not seen in shigellosis. Imaging and biopsy show transmural inflammation and granulomas.

confusedWithConditions.name: Foodborne Illness (general)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Most foodborne illness in adults is self-limited watery diarrhea from norovirus or toxin-producing bacteria, without the bloody dysenteric stools, high fever, and tenesmus that distinguish shigellosis.

relatedConditions.name: E. coli Infection
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares fecal-oral transmission and dysenteric presentation with Shiga toxin-producing strains; clinical and laboratory distinction is essential because treatment differs.

relatedConditions.name: Salmonella Infection
relatedConditions.relevance: Other major invasive bacterial enteric pathogen; differential diagnosis in any patient with fever and diarrhea.

relatedConditions.name: Amebiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Principal protozoal mimic of shigellosis in tropical regions and returning travelers; treatment with metronidazole differs from antibacterial therapy.

relatedConditions.name: Typhoid Fever
relatedConditions.relevance: Another invasive enteric infection with overlapping epidemiology; typhoid presents with prolonged fever and constipation more often than diarrhea, but co-circulates in endemic regions.

relatedConditions.name: Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Recognized complication of S. dysenteriae type 1 infection, mediated by Shiga toxin.

relatedConditions.name: Reactive Arthritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Post-infectious arthropathy occurring in 1-3% of shigellosis patients, especially HLA-B27 carriers.

relatedConditions.name: Foodborne Illness
relatedConditions.relevance: Shigella is one of the leading causes of foodborne diarrheal illness traced to infected handlers and contaminated produce.

faqs.question: How long is shigella contagious?
faqs.answer: People with shigellosis pass the bacteria in stool from symptom onset until 1-2 weeks after diarrhea stops. Most public health rules require staying home from work, school, or daycare for at least 48 hours after the last loose stool. Food handlers may need negative stool cultures.

faqs.question: How does shigella spread?
faqs.answer: Shigella spreads through the fecal-oral route. The infectious dose is very low (10-100 bacteria), so spread happens easily through unwashed hands, shared toys in daycare, contaminated food and water, sexual contact involving oral-anal exposure, and crowded shared bathrooms.

faqs.question: What antibiotics treat shigella?
faqs.answer: Azithromycin for 3 days is the first-line antibiotic globally due to rising ciprofloxacin resistance. Ciprofloxacin is an alternative where strains remain susceptible. Severe cases receive intravenous ceftriaxone for 5 days. Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole and ampicillin are no longer reliable.

faqs.question: How long does shigellosis last?
faqs.answer: Untreated, shigellosis typically lasts 5-7 days in healthy adults. With appropriate antibiotics, fever resolves in 24-48 hours and stool frequency improves over 3-5 days. Fatigue and reduced appetite can persist 1-2 weeks. Children and the elderly often take longer to recover.

faqs.question: Is shigellosis the same as food poisoning?
faqs.answer: Shigellosis is one type of food poisoning caused by Shigella bacteria. Other organisms — Salmonella, Campylobacter, E. coli, norovirus — also cause food poisoning. Shigella is distinguished by its very low infectious dose, bloody mucoid diarrhea, fever, and tenesmus.

faqs.question: Can I treat shigella at home?
faqs.answer: Mild shigellosis in healthy adults can often be managed at home with oral rehydration solution, rest, and bland food. Anyone with high fever, severe pain, bloody diarrhea, dehydration, pregnancy, immunocompromise, or at the age extremes should see a clinician promptly for antibiotics.

faqs.question: Should I take loperamide for shigella diarrhea?
faqs.answer: Loperamide and similar anti-diarrheal drugs should be avoided in dysenteric shigellosis. They can prolong invasive infection, raise risk of toxic megacolon, and may worsen hemolytic uremic syndrome with S. dysenteriae type 1. Use only under direct medical supervision.

faqs.question: What is the difference between shigella and E. coli?
faqs.answer: Both cause bloody diarrhea, but Shigella usually produces higher fever and more obvious tenesmus, while Shiga toxin-producing E. coli is more linked to hemolytic uremic syndrome from undercooked beef. Antibiotics help shigellosis but are generally avoided in STEC.

faqs.question: Is there a vaccine for shigella?
faqs.answer: There is no licensed Shigella vaccine as of 2026, but several candidates targeting S. sonnei and S. flexneri are in advanced clinical trials supported by WHO and major public health agencies. Routine vaccination is currently limited to rotavirus, a different diarrheal cause.

faqs.question: How is shigella diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is confirmed by stool testing. Stool culture is the traditional reference standard and provides antibiotic susceptibility data. Multiplex PCR panels detect Shigella DNA within hours and are widely used in high-income labs. Microscopy showing leukocytes and red cells supports diagnosis.

faqs.question: Can shigella come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Reinfection is possible because immunity is largely strain-specific, especially among the many S. flexneri serotypes. A short antibiotic course clears most infections, but a different Shigella strain at a later date can cause a new episode. Persistent carriage occurs in under 3% of patients.

faqs.question: Does shigella cause long-term problems?
faqs.answer: Most people recover fully. A minority develop post-infectious irritable bowel syndrome (5-15%) or reactive arthritis (1-3%, more common in HLA-B27 carriers). Hemolytic uremic syndrome with long-term kidney damage is rare and almost always linked to S. dysenteriae type 1.

faqs.question: Can children go to daycare with shigella?
faqs.answer: No. Children with confirmed or suspected shigellosis should be excluded from daycare until at least 48 hours after diarrhea stops. Many jurisdictions require negative stool cultures before return. Outbreaks spread rapidly due to the very low infectious dose.

faqs.question: How do I prevent shigella when traveling?
faqs.answer: Drink only bottled or boiled water, avoid ice from unknown sources, eat foods served hot, peel your own fruit, avoid raw produce washed in tap water, and wash hands frequently with soap or alcohol sanitizer. These steps also reduce risk of other travelers' diarrhea pathogens.

faqs.question: Is shigellosis a notifiable disease?
faqs.answer: Yes. Shigellosis is reportable to public health authorities in the US, UK, European Union, India, and most other countries. Reporting allows outbreak tracing, identification of resistance patterns, and intervention in shared exposure sources such as food, water, or daycare facilities.

faqs.question: Can I get shigella from sex?
faqs.answer: Yes. Sexually transmitted shigellosis is documented worldwide, particularly among men who have sex with men, through direct or indirect oral-anal contact. These clusters often involve multidrug-resistant strains. Barriers, hand washing, and abstaining during diarrheal illness reduce risk.

faqs.question: How serious is shigella in children?
faqs.answer: Shigellosis is among the leading bacterial causes of diarrheal death in children under 5, responsible for hundreds of thousands of deaths yearly, mainly in low- and middle-income countries. Severe dehydration, malnutrition, sepsis, and seizures drive most pediatric deaths.

faqs.question: What is dysentery?
faqs.answer: Dysentery is a clinical syndrome of frequent small-volume stools containing blood and mucus, with fever and painful straining (tenesmus). It is caused most often by Shigella (bacillary dysentery) or Entamoeba histolytica (amebic dysentery), though other invasive pathogens can produce it.

faqs.question: Why is shigella becoming antibiotic resistant?
faqs.answer: Heavy and inappropriate antibiotic use selects for resistant strains, and Shigella readily picks up resistance genes from plasmids shared among Enterobacteriaceae. Travel and sexual transmission spread resistant clones globally. WHO classifies it as a priority resistance pathogen.

faqs.question: Do I need follow-up testing after recovering from shigella?
faqs.answer: Most healthy adults do not need follow-up stool testing once symptoms resolve. Food handlers, healthcare workers, daycare staff, and children in daycare often need documented negative cultures before return. Persistent diarrhea beyond 2 weeks should be retested.
